title: Folk music

infobox.value: [List of folk music traditions](/wiki/List_of_folk_music_traditions "List of folk music traditions")
[List of folk musicians](/wiki/List_of_folk_musicians "List of folk musicians")

is a [music genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that includes [traditional folk music](#Traditional_folk_music) and the [contemporary genre](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") that evolved from the former during the 20th-century [folk revival](/wiki/Folk_revival "Folk revival"). Some types of folk music may be called [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"). Traditional folk music has been defined in several ways: as music transmitted orally, music with unknown composers, music that is played on traditional instruments, music about cultural or national identity, music that changes between generations (folk process), music associated with a people's [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), or music performed by [custom](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)") over a long period of time. It has been contrasted with [commercial](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and [classical styles](/wiki/Art_music "Art music"). The term originated in the 19th century, but folk music extends beyond that.

**Folk music** is a [music genre](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") that includes [traditional folk music](#Traditional_folk_music) and the [contemporary genre](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") that evolved from the former during the 20th-century [folk revival](/wiki/Folk_revival "Folk revival"). Some types of folk music may be called [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"). Traditional folk music has been defined in several ways: as music transmitted orally, music with unknown composers, music that is played on traditional instruments, music about cultural or national identity, music that changes between generations (folk process), music associated with a people's [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore"), or music performed by [custom](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)") over a long period of time. It has been contrasted with [commercial](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and [classical styles](/wiki/Art_music "Art music"). The term originated in the 19th century, but folk music extends beyond that.

Starting in the mid-20th century, a new form of popular folk music evolved from traditional folk music. This process and period is called the (second) folk revival and reached a zenith in the 1960s. This form of music is sometimes called [contemporary folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music") or folk revival music to distinguish it from earlier folk forms.[[1]](#cite_note-AboutDefinition-1) Smaller, similar revivals have occurred elsewhere in the world at other times, but the term folk music has typically not been applied to the new music created during those revivals. This type of folk music also includes fusion genres such as [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock"), [folk metal](/wiki/Folk_metal "Folk metal"), and others. While contemporary folk music is a genre generally distinct from traditional folk music, in [U.S. English](/wiki/American_English "American English") it shares the same name, and it often shares the same performers and venues as traditional folk music.

### Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

> Folk music is fascinating – in that just about everybody and their uncle has an opinion on what it is, and what it isn’t!

*[Goldmine](/wiki/Goldmine_(magazine) "Goldmine (magazine)")* (September 19, 2019)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The terms *folk music*, *folk song*, and *[folk dance](/wiki/Folk_dance "Folk dance")* are comparatively recent expressions. They are extensions of the term *[folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore")*, which was coined in 1846 by the English [antiquarian](/wiki/Antiquarian "Antiquarian") [William Thoms](/wiki/William_Thoms "William Thoms") to describe "the traditions, customs, and [superstitions](/wiki/Superstition "Superstition") of the uncultured classes".[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) The term further derives from the German expression *[Volk](/wiki/Volk "Volk")*, in the sense of "the people as a whole" as applied to popular and national music by [Johann Gottfried Herder](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Herder "Johann Gottfried Herder") and the [German Romantics](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism") over half a century earlier.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Though it is understood that folk music is the music of the people, observers find a more precise definition to be elusive.[[5]](#cite_note-NERevival-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Some do not even agree that the term folk music should be used.[[5]](#cite_note-NERevival-5) Folk music may tend to have certain characteristics[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) but it cannot clearly be differentiated in purely musical terms. One meaning often given is that of "old songs, with no known composers,"[[7]](#cite_note-Ronald_D._Cohen_2006_pp._1-2-7) another is that of music that has been submitted to an evolutionary "process of [oral transmission](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition")... the fashioning and re-fashioning of the music by the community that give it its folk character."[[8]](#cite_note-Lloyd_1969-8)

Such definitions depend upon "(cultural) processes rather than abstract musical types...", upon "*continuity* and *oral transmission*...seen as characterizing one side of a cultural [dichotomy](/wiki/Dichotomy "Dichotomy"), the other side of which is found not only in the lower layers of [feudal](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), [capitalist](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") and some oriental societies but also in 'primitive' societies and in parts of 'popular cultures'".[[9]](#cite_note-9) One widely used definition is simply "Folk music is what the people sing."[[10]](#cite_note-Donaldson,_2011_page_13-10)

For Scholes,[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) as well as for [Cecil Sharp](/wiki/Cecil_Sharp "Cecil Sharp") and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók"),[[11]](#cite_note-A.L.Lloyd,_1969,_pp._14-5-11) there was a sense of the music of the country as distinct from that of the town. Folk music was already, "...seen as the authentic expression of a way of life now past or about to disappear (or in some cases, to be preserved or somehow revived),"[[12]](#cite_note-12) particularly in "a community uninfluenced by art music"[[8]](#cite_note-Lloyd_1969-8) and by commercial and printed song. Lloyd rejected this in favor of a simple distinction of economic class[[11]](#cite_note-A.L.Lloyd,_1969,_pp._14-5-11) yet for him, true folk music was, in [Charles Seeger](/wiki/Charles_Seeger "Charles Seeger")'s words, "associated with a lower class"[[13]](#cite_note-Mills-13) in culturally and socially stratified societies. In these terms, folk music may be seen as part of a "schema comprising four musical types: 'primitive' or 'tribal'; 'elite' or 'art'; 'folk'; and 'popular'."[[14]](#cite_note-Charles_Seeger_1980_p.127-14)

Music in this genre is also often called "traditional music". Although the term is usually only descriptive, in some cases people use it as the name of a genre. For example, the [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") previously used the terms "traditional music" and "traditional folk" for folk music that is not contemporary folk music.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Folk music may include most [indigenous music](/wiki/Indigenous_music "Indigenous music").[[5]](#cite_note-NERevival-5)

#### Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Viljandi_2010_002.jpg/250px-Viljandi_2010_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viljandi_2010_002.jpg)

[Viljandi Folk Music Festival](/wiki/Viljandi_Folk_Music_Festival "Viljandi Folk Music Festival") held annually within the castle ruins in [Viljandi](/wiki/Viljandi "Viljandi"), Estonia

From a historical perspective, traditional folk music had these characteristics:[[13]](#cite_note-Mills-13)

* It was transmitted through an [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"). Before the 20th century, ordinary people were usually [illiterate](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") and learned songs by memory. Primarily, this was not mediated by books or recorded or transmitted media. Singers may have extended their repertoire using [broadsheets](/wiki/Broadside_(music) "Broadside (music)") or [song books](/wiki/Song_book "Song book"), but these secondary enhancements are of the same character as the primary songs experienced in the flesh.
* The music was often related to [national culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"). It was culturally particular; from a particular region or culture. In the context of an immigrant group, folk music acquires an extra dimension for social cohesion. It is particularly conspicuous in immigrant societies, where [Greek Australians](/wiki/Greek_Australians "Greek Australians"), [Somali Americans](/wiki/Somali_Americans "Somali Americans"), [Punjabi Canadians](/wiki/Punjabi_Canadians "Punjabi Canadians"), and others strive to emphasize their cultural identity. They learn songs and dances that originate in the countries their grandparents came from.
* They commemorate historical and personal events. On certain days of the year, including such holidays as Christmas, Easter, and May Day, particular songs celebrate the yearly cycle. Birthdays, weddings, and funerals may also be noted with songs, dances and special costumes. Religious festivals often have a folk music component. [Choral music](/wiki/Choral_music "Choral music") at these events brings children and non-professional singers to participate in a public arena, giving an emotional bonding that is unrelated to the aesthetic qualities of the music.
* The songs have been performed, by [custom](/wiki/Convention_(norm) "Convention (norm)"), over a long period of time, usually several [generations](/wiki/Generation "Generation").

As a side-effect, the following characteristics are sometimes present:

* There is no [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") on the songs. Hundreds of folk songs from the 19th century have known authors but have continued in oral tradition to the point where they are considered traditional for purposes of music publishing. This has become much less frequent since the 1940s. Today, almost every folk song that is recorded is credited with an arranger.
* Fusion of cultures: Because cultures interact and change over time, traditional songs evolving over time may incorporate and reflect influences from disparate cultures. The relevant factors may include [instrumentation](/wiki/Instrumentation_(music) "Instrumentation (music)"), [tunings](/wiki/Musical_tuning "Musical tuning"), [voicings](/wiki/Voicing_(music) "Voicing (music)"), [phrasing](/wiki/Phrasing_(music) "Phrasing (music)"), subject matter, and even production methods.
* The accompanying chords and harmony parts for a song would be improvised. Broadsheets and song books contained limited detail (if any) about the melody and harmony for a given song, so performers would make their own choices and compose spontaneous arrangements. This is in contrast to the detailed, prescriptive sheet music used to transcribe classical music compositions at the time.

### Tune

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tune")]

In folk music, a *tune* is a short [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental "Instrumental") [piece](/wiki/Piece_(music) "Piece (music)"), a [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody"), often with repeating [sections](/wiki/Section_(music) "Section (music)"), and usually played a number of times.[[16]](#cite_note-16) A collection of tunes with structural similarities is known as a [tune-family](/wiki/Tune-family "Tune-family"). *America's Musical Landscape*[[17]](#cite_note-17) says "the most common [form](/wiki/Form_(music) "Form (music)") for tunes in folk music is AABB, also known as [binary form](/wiki/Binary_form "Binary form")."[[18]](#cite_note-18)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In some traditions, tunes may be strung together in [medleys](/wiki/Medley_(music) "Medley (music)") or sets.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Studies demonstrate that specific traditional music patterns from European musical heritage follow mathematical [sequences](/wiki/Sequence "Sequence") helping traditional songs survive through oral passing of melodies between generations.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Indian_village_musicians.jpg/250px-Indian_village_musicians.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_village_musicians.jpg)

Indians always distinguished between classical and folk music, though in the past even classical Indian music used to rely on the unwritten transmission of repertoire.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Navneet-Aditya-Waiba-Live.jpg/250px-Navneet-Aditya-Waiba-Live.jpg)](/wiki/File:Navneet-Aditya-Waiba-Live.jpg)

Indian Nepali folk musician [Navneet Aditya Waiba](/wiki/Navneet_Aditya_Waiba "Navneet Aditya Waiba")

Throughout most of human history, listening to [recorded music](/wiki/Recorded_music "Recorded music") was not possible.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Music was made by common people during both their work and leisure, as well as during religious activities. The work of economic production was often manual and communal.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Manual labor](/wiki/Manual_labor "Manual labor") often included singing by the workers, which served several practical purposes.[[24]](#cite_note-24) It reduced the [boredom](/wiki/Boredom "Boredom") of repetitive tasks, it kept the [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm") during [synchronized](/wiki/Synchronization "Synchronization") pushes and pulls, and it set the pace of many activities such as [planting](/wiki/Sowing "Sowing"), [weeding](/wiki/Weeding "Weeding"), [reaping](/wiki/Reaping "Reaping"), [threshing](/wiki/Threshing "Threshing"), [weaving](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving"), and [milling](/wiki/Milling_(grinding) "Milling (grinding)"). In [leisure time](/wiki/Leisure_time "Leisure time"), singing and playing [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") were common forms of entertainment and history-telling—even more common than today when electrically enabled technologies and widespread literacy make other forms of entertainment and information-sharing competitive.[[25]](#cite_note-SeegerLomaxDocumentary-25)

Some believe that folk music originated as [art music](/wiki/Art_music "Art music") that was changed and probably debased by oral transmission while reflecting the character of the society that produced it.[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) In many societies, especially preliterate ones, the cultural transmission of folk music requires [learning by ear](/wiki/Learning_music_by_ear "Learning music by ear"), although [notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation") has evolved in some cultures.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Different cultures may have different notions concerning a division between "folk" music on the one hand and of "art" and "court" music on the other. In the proliferation of popular music genres, some traditional folk music became also referred to as "[World music](/wiki/World_music "World music")" or "Roots music".[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The English term "[folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore")", to describe traditional folk music and dance, entered the vocabulary of many continental European nations, each of which had its folk-song collectors and revivalists.[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) The distinction between "authentic" folk and national and [popular song](/wiki/Popular_song "Popular song") in general has always been loose, particularly in America and Germany[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3) – for example, popular songwriters such as [Stephen Foster](/wiki/Stephen_Foster "Stephen Foster") could be termed "folk" in America.[[3]](#cite_note-Percy_Scholes_1977-3)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The [International Folk Music Council](/wiki/International_Folk_Music_Council "International Folk Music Council") definition allows that the term can also apply to music that, "...has originated with an individual composer and has subsequently been absorbed into the unwritten, living tradition of a community. But the term does not cover a song, dance, or tune that has been taken over ready-made and remains unchanged."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The post–[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") folk revival in America and in Britain started a new genre, [contemporary folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music"), and brought an additional meaning to the term "folk music": newly composed songs, fixed in form and by known authors, which imitated some form of traditional music. The popularity of "contemporary folk" recordings caused the appearance of the category "Folk" in the [Grammy Awards](/wiki/Grammy_Award "Grammy Award") of 1959;[[30]](#cite_note-30) in 1970 the term was dropped in favor of "Best Ethnic or Traditional Recording (including Traditional Blues)",[[31]](#cite_note-31) while 1987 brought a distinction between "Best Traditional Folk Recording" and "Best Contemporary Folk Recording".[[32]](#cite_note-32) After that, they had a "Traditional music" category that subsequently evolved into others. The term "folk", by the start of the 21st century, could cover singer-songwriters, such as [Donovan](/wiki/Donovan "Donovan")[[33]](#cite_note-33) from Scotland and [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan")[[34]](#cite_note-34) from the United States, who emerged in the 1960s, and much more. This completed a process to where "folk music" no longer meant only traditional folk music.[[7]](#cite_note-Ronald_D._Cohen_2006_pp._1-2-7)

### Subject matter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Subject matter")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Ahmet_Kaya.jpg/250px-Ahmet_Kaya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahmet_Kaya.jpg)

[Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_culture "Turkish culture") traditional folk singer [Ahmet Kaya](/wiki/Ahmet_Kaya "Ahmet Kaya")

[![Assyrian folk music](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/6a/Assyrianfolk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assyrianfolk.jpg)

[Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") playing a *[zurna](/wiki/Zurna "Zurna")* and a *[davul](/wiki/Davul "Davul")*, instruments typically used for Assyrian folk music and [dance](/wiki/Assyrian_folk_dance "Assyrian folk dance")

Traditional folk music often includes [sung words](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics"), although folk [instrumental music](/wiki/Instrumental_music "Instrumental music") occurs commonly in [dance music](/wiki/Dancing_music "Dancing music") traditions. [Narrative verse](/wiki/Narrative_verse "Narrative verse") looms large in the traditional folk music of many cultures.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) This encompasses such forms as traditional [epic poetry](/wiki/Epic_poetry "Epic poetry"), much of which was meant originally for oral performance, sometimes accompanied by instruments.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Many epic poems of various cultures were pieced together from shorter pieces of traditional narrative verse, which explains their episodic structure, repetitive elements, and their frequent *[in medias res](/wiki/In_medias_res "In medias res")* plot developments. Other forms of traditional narrative verse relate the outcomes of [battles](/wiki/Battle "Battle") or lament [tragedies](/wiki/Tragedy_(event) "Tragedy (event)") or [natural disasters](/wiki/Natural_disaster "Natural disaster").[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Sometimes, as in the triumphant *[Song of Deborah](/wiki/Song_of_Deborah "Song of Deborah")*[[40]](#cite_note-40) found in the [Biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") *[Book of Judges](/wiki/Book_of_Judges "Book of Judges")*, these songs celebrate victory. Laments for lost battles and wars, and the lives lost in them, are equally prominent in many traditions; these laments keep alive the cause for which the battle was fought.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) The narratives of traditional songs often also remember [folk heroes](/wiki/Folk_hero "Folk hero") such as [John Henry](/wiki/John_Henry_(folklore) "John Henry (folklore)")[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) or [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Some traditional song narratives recall [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") events or mysterious deaths.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[Hymns](/wiki/Hymn "Hymn") and other forms of [religious music](/wiki/Religious_music "Religious music") are often of traditional and unknown origin.[[47]](#cite_note-47) Western [musical notation](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation") was originally created to preserve the lines of [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant"), which before its invention was taught as an oral tradition in [monastic](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") communities.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) Traditional songs such as *[Green grow the rushes, O](/wiki/Green_grow_the_rushes,_O "Green grow the rushes, O")* present religious lore in a [mnemonic](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic") form, as do Western [Christmas carols](/wiki/Christmas_carol "Christmas carol") and similar traditional songs.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

[Work songs](/wiki/Work_song "Work song") frequently feature [call and response](/wiki/Call_and_response_(music) "Call and response (music)") structures and are designed to enable the laborers who sing them to coordinate their efforts in accordance with the rhythms of the songs.[[51]](#cite_note-51) In the American [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_force "Armed force"), a lively oral tradition preserves [jody calls](/wiki/Jody_call "Jody call") ("Duckworth chants") which are sung while soldiers are on the march.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Professional sailors made similar use of a large body of [sea shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty").[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) [Love poetry](/wiki/Love_poetry "Love poetry"), often of a tragic or regretful nature, prominently figures in many folk traditions.[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Nursery rhymes](/wiki/Nursery_rhyme "Nursery rhyme"), [children's songs](/wiki/Children%27s_song "Children's song") and [nonsense verse](/wiki/Nonsense_verse "Nonsense verse") used to amuse or quiet children also are frequent subjects of traditional songs.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Music transmitted by word of mouth through a community, in time, develops many variants, since this transmission cannot produce word-for-word and note-for-note accuracy. In addition, folk singers can choose to modify the songs they hear.

For example, around 1970 the song *Mullā Mohammed Jān* spread from [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") to the rest of [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") where it was recorded. Due to its repetitive refrain and the predictability of the second half of each [verse](/wiki/Metre_(poetry) "Metre (poetry)"), it allowed for both its popularization, and for each singer to create their own version of the song without being overly-concerned for a [melody](/wiki/Melody "Melody") or restrictive poetic rhythm.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Because variants proliferate naturally, there is generally no "authoritative" version of song. Researchers in traditional songs have encountered countless versions of the [Barbara Allen](/wiki/Barbara_Allen_(song) "Barbara Allen (song)") ballad throughout the English-speaking world, and these versions often differ greatly from each other. The original is not known; many versions lay an equal claim to authenticity.

Influential folklorist [Cecil Sharp](/wiki/Cecil_Sharp "Cecil Sharp") felt that these competing variants of a traditional song would undergo a process of improvement akin to biological [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection"): only those new variants that were the most appealing to ordinary singers would be picked up by others and transmitted onward in time. Thus, over time we would expect each traditional song to become more aesthetically appealing, due to incremental community improvement.

Literary interest in the popular ballad form dates back at least to [Thomas Percy](/wiki/Thomas_Percy_(bishop_of_Dromore) "Thomas Percy (bishop of Dromore)") and [William Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth"). English Elizabethan and Stuart composers had often evolved their music from folk themes, the classical [suite](/wiki/Suite_(music) "Suite (music)") was based upon stylised folk-dances, and [Joseph Haydn](/wiki/Haydn_and_folk_music "Haydn and folk music")'s use of folk melodies is noted. But the emergence of the term "folk" coincided with an "outburst of national feeling all over Europe" that was particularly strong at the edges of Europe, where [national identity](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") was most asserted. [Nationalist composers](/wiki/Musical_nationalism "Musical nationalism") emerged in Central Europe, Russia, Scandinavia, Spain and Britain: the music of [Dvořák](/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k "Antonín Dvořák"), [Smetana](/wiki/Bed%C5%99ich_Smetana "Bedřich Smetana"), [Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg"), [Rimsky-Korsakov](/wiki/Rimsky-Korsakov "Rimsky-Korsakov"), [Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms"), [Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt"), [de Falla](/wiki/Manuel_de_Falla "Manuel de Falla"), [Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner"), [Sibelius](/wiki/Jean_Sibelius "Jean Sibelius"), [Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams"), [Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók"), and many others drew upon folk melodies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Regional forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Regional forms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Naxi_Musicians_I.jpg/250px-Naxi_Musicians_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naxi_Musicians_I.jpg)

[Naxi](/wiki/Nakhi_people "Nakhi people") traditional musicians

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/015_Die_Br%C3%BCder_Steinegger_nach_einem_Lichtbild_in_den_1880er-Jahren_-_%C3%84nderung_der_Tracht.jpg/250px-015_Die_Br%C3%BCder_Steinegger_nach_einem_Lichtbild_in_den_1880er-Jahren_-_%C3%84nderung_der_Tracht.jpg)](/wiki/File:015_Die_Br%C3%BCder_Steinegger_nach_einem_Lichtbild_in_den_1880er-Jahren_-_%C3%84nderung_der_Tracht.jpg)

The Steinegger brothers, traditional fifers of [Grundlsee](/wiki/Grundlsee "Grundlsee"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), 1880

While the loss of traditional folk music in the face of the rise of [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") is a worldwide phenomenon,[[59]](#cite_note-59) it is not one occurring at a uniform rate throughout the world.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The process is most advanced "where industrialization and [commercialisation](/wiki/Commercialization "Commercialization") of culture are most advanced"[[61]](#cite_note-61) but also occurs more gradually even in settings of lower technological advancement. However, the loss of traditional music is slowed in nations or regions where traditional folk music is a badge of cultural or national identity.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Ethnomusicologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology") notes that the [Intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage") program organized by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") stands among the principal measures implemented by governments which protect regional music traditions from disappearing.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Early folk music, fieldwork and scholarship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early folk music, fieldwork and scholarship")]

Much of what is known about folk music prior to the development of audio recording technology in the 19th century comes from [fieldwork](/wiki/Field_research "Field research") and writings of scholars, collectors and proponents.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

#### 19th-century Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 19th-century Europe")]

Starting in the 19th century, academics and amateur scholars, taking note of the musical traditions being lost, initiated various efforts to preserve the music of the people.[[64]](#cite_note-64) One such effort was the collection by [Francis James Child](/wiki/Francis_James_Child "Francis James Child") in the late 19th century of the texts of over three hundred [ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad") in the English and Scots traditions (called the [Child Ballads](/wiki/Child_Ballads "Child Ballads")), some of which predated the 16th century.[[10]](#cite_note-Donaldson,_2011_page_13-10)

Contemporaneously with Child, the Reverend [Sabine Baring-Gould](/wiki/Sabine_Baring-Gould "Sabine Baring-Gould") and later [Cecil Sharp](/wiki/Cecil_Sharp "Cecil Sharp") worked to preserve a great body of English rural traditional song, music and dance, under the aegis of what became and remains the [English Folk Dance and Song Society](/wiki/English_Folk_Dance_and_Song_Society "English Folk Dance and Song Society") (EFDSS).[[65]](#cite_note-65) Sharp campaigned with some success to have English traditional songs (in his own heavily edited and expurgated versions) to be taught to school children in hopes of reviving and prolonging the popularity of those songs.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Throughout the 1960s and early to mid-1970s, American scholar [Bertrand Harris Bronson](/wiki/Bertrand_Harris_Bronson "Bertrand Harris Bronson") published an exhaustive four-volume collection of the then-known variations of both the texts and tunes associated with what came to be known as the Child Canon.[[68]](#cite_note-68) He also advanced some significant theories concerning the workings of oral-aural tradition.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Similar activity was also under way in other countries. One of the most extensive was perhaps the work done in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") by [Krišjānis Barons](/wiki/Kri%C5%A1j%C4%81nis_Barons "Krišjānis Barons"), who between the years 1894 and 1915 published six volumes that included the texts of 217,996 [Latvian folk songs](/wiki/Daina_(Latvia) "Daina (Latvia)"), the *Latvju dainas*, which he stored in the [Cabinet of Folksongs](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Folksongs "Cabinet of Folksongs").[[70]](#cite_note-70) In Norway the work of collectors such as [Ludvig Mathias Lindeman](/wiki/Ludvig_Mathias_Lindeman "Ludvig Mathias Lindeman") was extensively used by Edvard Grieg in his *Lyric Pieces* for piano and in other works, which became immensely popular.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Around this time, composers of [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music") developed a strong interest in collecting traditional songs, and a number of composers carried out their own field work on traditional music. These included [Percy Grainger](/wiki/Percy_Grainger "Percy Grainger")[[72]](#cite_note-72) and [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams")[[73]](#cite_note-73) in England and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók")[[74]](#cite_note-74) in Hungary. These composers, like many of their predecessors, both made arrangements of folk songs and incorporated traditional material into original classical compositions.[[75]](#cite_note-75)[[76]](#cite_note-76)

#### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: North America")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/31/Cecil-sharp-appalachia-map.png)](/wiki/File:Cecil-sharp-appalachia-map.png)

Locations in Southern and Central [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") visited by the British folklorist [Cecil Sharp](/wiki/Cecil_Sharp "Cecil Sharp") in 1916 (blue), 1917 (green), and 1918 (red). Sharp sought "old world" English and Scottish ballads passed down to the region's inhabitants from their British ancestors. He collected hundreds of such ballads, the most productive areas being the [Blue Ridge Mountains](/wiki/Blue_Ridge_Mountains "Blue Ridge Mountains") of North Carolina and the [Cumberland Mountains](/wiki/Cumberland_Mountains "Cumberland Mountains") of Kentucky.

The advent of [audio recording](/wiki/Audio_recording "Audio recording") technology provided folklorists with a revolutionary tool to preserve vanishing musical forms.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The earliest American folk music scholars were with the [American Folklore Society](/wiki/American_Folklore_Society "American Folklore Society") (AFS), which emerged in the late 1800s.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Their studies expanded to include [Native American music](/wiki/Indigenous_music_of_North_America "Indigenous music of North America"), but still treated folk music as a historical item preserved in isolated societies as well.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In North America, during the 1930s and 1940s, the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") worked through the offices of traditional music collectors [Robert Winslow Gordon](/wiki/Robert_Winslow_Gordon "Robert Winslow Gordon"),[[80]](#cite_note-80) [Alan Lomax](/wiki/Alan_Lomax "Alan Lomax")[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) and others to capture as much North American field material as possible.[[84]](#cite_note-84) [John Lomax](/wiki/John_Lomax "John Lomax") (the father of Alan Lomax) was the first prominent scholar to study distinctly American folk music such as that of cowboys and southern blacks. His first major published work was in 1911, *Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads*.[[85]](#cite_note-85) John Lomax was arguably the most prominent US folk music scholar of his time, notably during the beginnings of the folk music revival in the 1930s and early 1940s. Cecil Sharp also worked in America, recording the traditional songs of the Appalachian Mountains in 1916–1918 in collaboration with [Maud Karpeles](/wiki/Maud_Karpeles "Maud Karpeles") and [Olive Dame Campbell](/wiki/Olive_Dame_Campbell "Olive Dame Campbell"), and is considered the first major scholar covering American folk music.[[86]](#cite_note-86) Campbell and Sharp are represented under other names by actors in the modern movie *[Songcatcher](/wiki/Songcatcher "Songcatcher")*.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

One strong theme amongst folk scholars in the early decades of the 20th century was [regionalism](/wiki/Regionalism_(art) "Regionalism (art)"),[[88]](#cite_note-88) the analysis of the diversity of folk music (and related cultures) based on regions of the US rather than based on a given song's historical roots.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90) Later, a dynamic of class and circumstances was added to this.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The most prominent regionalists were literary figures with a particular interest in folklore.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) [Carl Sandburg](/wiki/Carl_Sandburg "Carl Sandburg") often traveled the U.S. as a writer and a poet.[[94]](#cite_note-94) He also collected songs in his travels and, in 1927, published them in the book *[The American Songbag](/wiki/The_American_Songbag "The American Songbag")*.[[95]](#cite_note-95) Rachel Donaldson, a historian who worked for Vanderbilt, later stated this about *The American Songbag* in her analysis of the folk music revival: "In his collections of folk songs, Sandburg added a class dynamic to popular understandings of American folk music. This was the final element of the foundation upon which the early folk music revivalists constructed their own view of Americanism. Sandburg's working-class Americans joined with the [ethnically](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group"), [racially](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)"), and regionally diverse citizens that other scholars, public intellectuals, and folklorists celebrated their own definitions of the American folk, definitions that the folk revivalists used in constructing their own understanding of American folk music, and an overarching American identity."[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Prior to the 1930s, the study of folk music was primarily the province of scholars and collectors. The 1930s saw the beginnings of larger scale themes, commonalities, and linkages in folk music developing in the populace and practitioners as well, often related to the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[97]](#cite_note-Donaldson,_2011,_p_39-55-97) Regionalism and [cultural pluralism](/wiki/Cultural_pluralism "Cultural pluralism") grew as influences and themes. During this time folk music began to become enmeshed with political and social activism themes and movements.[[97]](#cite_note-Donaldson,_2011,_p_39-55-97) Two related developments were the U.S. Communist Party's interest in folk music as a way to reach and influence Americans,[[98]](#cite_note-98) and politically active prominent folk musicians and scholars seeing communism as a possible better system, through the lens of the Great Depression.[[99]](#cite_note-99) [Woody Guthrie](/wiki/Woody_Guthrie "Woody Guthrie") exemplifies songwriters and artists with such an outlook.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Folk music festivals proliferated during the 1930s.[[101]](#cite_note-101) President [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Roosevelt "Franklin Roosevelt") was a fan of folk music, hosted folk concerts at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), and often patronized folk festivals.[[102]](#cite_note-102) One prominent festival was [Sarah Gertrude Knott](/wiki/Sarah_Gertrude_Knott "Sarah Gertrude Knott")'s [National Folk Festival](/wiki/National_Folk_Festival_(United_States) "National Folk Festival (United States)"), established in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), Missouri, in 1934.[[103]](#cite_note-103) Under the sponsorship of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")*, the festival was held in Washington, DC, at [Constitution Hall](/wiki/DAR_Constitution_Hall "DAR Constitution Hall") from 1937 to 1942.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The folk music movement, festivals, and the wartime effort were seen as forces for social goods such as democracy, cultural pluralism, and the removal of culture and race-based barriers.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

The American folk music revivalists of the 1930s approached folk music in different ways.[[106]](#cite_note-Donaldson87-106) Three primary schools of thought emerged: "Traditionalists" (e.g. Sarah Gertrude Knott and [John Lomax](/wiki/John_Lomax "John Lomax")) emphasized the preservation of songs as artifacts of deceased cultures. "Functional" folklorists (e.g. Botkin and Alan Lomax) maintained that songs only retain relevance when used by those cultures which retain the traditions which birthed those songs. "Left-wing" folk revivalists (e.g. Charles Seeger and Lawrence Gellert) emphasized music's role "in 'people's' struggles for social and political rights".[[106]](#cite_note-Donaldson87-106) By the end of the 1930s these and others had turned American folk music into a social movement.[[106]](#cite_note-Donaldson87-106)

Sometimes folk musicians became scholars and advocates themselves. For example, [Jean Ritchie](/wiki/Jean_Ritchie "Jean Ritchie") (1922–2015) was the youngest child of a large family from [Viper, Kentucky](/wiki/Viper,_Kentucky "Viper, Kentucky"), that had preserved many of the old [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") traditional songs.[[107]](#cite_note-107) Ritchie, living in a time when the [Appalachians](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") had opened up to outside influence, was university educated and ultimately moved to New York City, where she made a number of classic recordings of the family repertoire and published an important compilation of these songs.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

In January 2012, the [American Folklife Center](/wiki/American_Folklife_Center "American Folklife Center") at the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress"), with the Association for Cultural Equity, announced that they would release Lomax's vast archive of 1946 and later recording in digital form. Lomax spent the last 20 years of his life working on an [interactive multimedia](/wiki/Interactive_multimedia "Interactive multimedia") educational computer project he called the [Global Jukebox](/wiki/Cantometrics#Cantometrics_as_an_educational_tool "Cantometrics"), which included 5,000 hours of sound recordings, 400,000 feet of film, 3,000 [videotapes](/wiki/Videotape "Videotape"), and 5,000 photographs.[[109]](#cite_note-109) As of March 2012, this had been accomplished. Approximately 17,400 of Lomax's recordings from 1946 and later have been made available free online.[[110]](#cite_note-ACEmusic-110)[[111]](#cite_note-NPR17000-111) This material from Alan Lomax's independent archive, begun in 1946, which has been digitized and offered by the Association for Cultural Equity, is "distinct from the thousands of earlier recordings on acetate and aluminum discs he made from 1933 to 1942 under the auspices of the Library of Congress. This earlier collection—which includes the famous [Jelly Roll Morton](/wiki/Jelly_Roll_Morton "Jelly Roll Morton"), Woody Guthrie, [Lead Belly](/wiki/Lead_Belly "Lead Belly"), and [Muddy Waters](/wiki/Muddy_Waters "Muddy Waters") sessions, as well as Lomax's prodigious collections made in [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and Eastern Kentucky (1937) — is the provenance of the American Folklife Center"[[110]](#cite_note-ACEmusic-110) at the Library of Congress.

### National and regional forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: National and regional forms")]

#### Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Africa")]

Main article: [Music of Africa](/wiki/Music_of_Africa "Music of Africa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Mbira_dzavadzimu.jpg/250px-Mbira_dzavadzimu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mbira_dzavadzimu.jpg)

The African [lamellophone](/wiki/Lamellophone "Lamellophone"), [thumb piano](/wiki/Thumb_piano "Thumb piano") or [mbira](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira")

Africa is a vast continent[[112]](#cite_note-112) and its [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Africa "Regions of Africa") and [nations](/wiki/List_of_African_countries "List of African countries") have distinct musical traditions.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) The [music of North Africa](/wiki/List_of_Middle_Eastern_and_North_African_folk_music_traditions "List of Middle Eastern and North African folk music traditions") for the most part has a different history from [Sub-Saharan African music traditions](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_African_music_traditions "Sub-Saharan African music traditions").[[115]](#cite_note-115)

The music and dance forms of the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora"), including [African American music](/wiki/African_American_music "African American music") and many [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_music "Caribbean music") genres like [soca](/wiki/Soca_music "Soca music"), [calypso](/wiki/Calypso_music "Calypso music") and [Zouk](/wiki/Zouk_(musical_movement) "Zouk (musical movement)"); and [Latin American music](/wiki/Latin_American_music "Latin American music") genres like the [samba](/wiki/Samba_(music) "Samba (music)"), [Cuban rumba](/wiki/Cuban_rumba "Cuban rumba"), [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music"); and other [clave (rhythm)](/wiki/Clave_(rhythm) "Clave (rhythm)")-based genres, were founded to varying degrees on the music of [enslaved Africans](/wiki/Slavery_in_Africa "Slavery in Africa"), which has in turn influenced [African popular music](/wiki/African_popular_music "African popular music").[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

#### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Asia")]

See also: [Tamang Selo](/wiki/Tamang_Selo "Tamang Selo"), [Iranian folk music](/wiki/Iranian_folk_music "Iranian folk music"), [Indian folk music](/wiki/Indian_folk_music "Indian folk music"), and [Korean folk music](/wiki/Korean_folk_music "Korean folk music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Paban_Das_Baul_at_Nine_Lives_concert%2C_2009.jpg/250px-Paban_Das_Baul_at_Nine_Lives_concert%2C_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paban_Das_Baul_at_Nine_Lives_concert,_2009.jpg)

[Paban Das Baul](/wiki/Paban_Das_Baul "Paban Das Baul"), [baul](/wiki/Baul "Baul") singer at Nine Lives concert, 2009

Many Asian civilizations distinguish between [art](/wiki/Art_music "Art music")/court/classical styles and "folk" music.[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) For example, the late [Alam Lohar](/wiki/Alam_Lohar "Alam Lohar") is an example of a South Asian singer who was classified as a folk singer.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

Khunung Eshei/Khuland Eshei is an ancient folk song from India, a country of Asia, of [Meiteis](/wiki/Meitei_people "Meitei people") of [Manipur](/wiki/Manipur "Manipur"), that is an example of Asian folk music, and how they put it into its own genre.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

##### Folk music of China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Folk music of China")]

Further information: [Music of China § Folk music](/wiki/Music_of_China#Folk_music "Music of China"), [Shijing](/wiki/Shijing "Shijing"), and [Yuefu](/wiki/Yuefu "Yuefu")

Archaeological discoveries date [Chinese](/wiki/Culture_of_China "Culture of China") folk music back 7000 years;[[122]](#cite_note-122) it is largely based on the [pentatonic scale](/wiki/Pentatonic_scale "Pentatonic scale").[[123]](#cite_note-123)

[Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") traditional weddings and funerals usually include a form of oboe called a [suona](/wiki/Suona "Suona"),[[124]](#cite_note-124) and apercussive ensembles called a [chuigushou](/wiki/Chuigushou "Chuigushou").[[125]](#cite_note-125) Ensembles consisting of [mouth organs](/wiki/Mouth_organ "Mouth organ") ([sheng](/wiki/Sheng_(instrument) "Sheng (instrument)")), shawms ([suona](/wiki/Suona "Suona")), flutes ([dizi](/wiki/Dizi_(instrument) "Dizi (instrument)")) and percussion instruments (especially [yunluo](/wiki/Yunluo "Yunluo") [gongs](/wiki/Gong "Gong")) are popular in northern villages;[[126]](#cite_note-126) their music is descended from the imperial temple music of Beijing, [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), [Wutai shan](/wiki/Wutai_shan "Wutai shan") and [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin"). [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an") drum music, consisting of wind and percussive instruments,[[127]](#cite_note-127) is popular around Xi'an, and has received some commercial popularity outside of China.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Another important instrument is the [sheng](/wiki/Sheng_(instrument) "Sheng (instrument)"), a type of Chinese [pipe](/wiki/Chinese_flute "Chinese flute"), an ancient instrument that is ancestor of all Western [free reed](/wiki/Free_reed "Free reed") instruments, such as the [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion").[[129]](#cite_note-129) Parades led by Western-type [brass bands](/wiki/Brass_band "Brass band") are common, often competing in volume with a shawm/chuigushou band.

In southern [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), Nanyin or [Nanguan](/wiki/Nanguan_music "Nanguan music") is a genre of traditional ballads.[[130]](#cite_note-130) They are sung by a woman accompanied by a [xiao](/wiki/Xiao_(flute) "Xiao (flute)") and a [pipa](/wiki/Pipa "Pipa"), as well as other traditional instruments.[[131]](#cite_note-131) The music is generally sorrowful and typically deals with love-stricken people.[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) Further south, in [Shantou](/wiki/Shantou "Shantou"), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_people "Hakka people") and [Chaozhou](/wiki/Chaozhou "Chaozhou"), [zheng](/wiki/Guzheng "Guzheng") ensembles are popular.[[134]](#cite_note-134) [Sizhu](/wiki/Sizhu "Sizhu") ensembles use flutes and bowed or plucked string instruments to make harmonious and melodious music that has become popular in the West among some listeners.[[135]](#cite_note-135) These are popular in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing") and [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), as well as elsewhere along the southern [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze_River "Yangtze River") area.[[136]](#cite_note-136) [Jiangnan Sizhu](/wiki/Jiangnan_Sizhu "Jiangnan Sizhu") (silk and bamboo music from [Jiangnan](/wiki/Jiangnan "Jiangnan")) is a style of instrumental music, often played by amateur musicians in tea houses in Shanghai.[[137]](#cite_note-137) [Guangdong Music](/wiki/Guangdong_music_(genre) "Guangdong music (genre)") or [Cantonese Music](/wiki/Guangdong_music_(genre) "Guangdong music (genre)") is instrumental music from [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou") and surrounding areas.[[138]](#cite_note-138) The music from this region influenced [Yueju](/wiki/Cantonese_Opera "Cantonese Opera") (Cantonese Opera) music,[[139]](#cite_note-139) which would later grow popular during the self-described "Golden Age" of China under the [PRC](/wiki/China "China").[[140]](#cite_note-140)

Folk songs have been recorded since ancient times in China. The term [Yuefu](/wiki/Yuefu "Yuefu") was used for a broad range of songs such as ballads, laments, folk songs, love songs, and songs performed at court.[[141]](#cite_note-141) China is a vast country, with a multiplicity of linguistic and geographic regions. Folk songs are categorized by geographic region, language type, ethnicity, social function (e.g. work song, ritual song, courting song) and musical type. Modern anthologies collected by Chinese folklorists distinguish between traditional songs, revolutionary songs, and newly invented songs.[[142]](#cite_note-142) The songs of northwest China are known as "flower songs" (*hua'er*), a reference to beautiful women, while in the past they were notorious for their erotic content.[[143]](#cite_note-143) The village "mountain songs" (*[shan'ge](/wiki/Shan%27ge "Shan'ge")*) of Jiangsu province were also well known for their amorous themes.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) Other regional song traditions include the "strummed lyrics" (*[tanci](/wiki/Tanci "Tanci")*) of the Lower Yangtze Delta, the Cantonese Wooden Fish tradition (*muyu* or *muk-yu*) and the Drum Songs (*[guci](/wiki/Guci "Guci")*) of north China.[[146]](#cite_note-146)

In the twenty-first century many cherished Chinese folk songs have been inscribed in the [UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists").[[147]](#cite_note-147) In the process, songs once seen as vulgar are now being reconstructed as romantic courtship songs.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Regional song competitions, popular in many communities, have promoted professional folk singing as a career, with some individual folk singers having gained national prominence.[[149]](#cite_note-149)

##### Traditional folk music of Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Traditional folk music of Sri Lanka")]

Main article: [Music of Sri Lanka](/wiki/Music_of_Sri_Lanka "Music of Sri Lanka")

The art, music and dance of Sri Lanka derive from the elements of nature, and have been enjoyed and developed in the [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") environment.[[150]](#cite_note-150) The music is of several types and uses only a few types of instruments.[[151]](#cite_note-151) The folk songs and poems were used in social gatherings to work together. The Indian influenced classical music has grown to be unique.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)[[154]](#cite_note-154)[[155]](#cite_note-155) The traditional drama, music and songs of Sinhala Light Music are typically Sri Lankan.[[156]](#cite_note-156)
The temple paintings and carvings feature birds, elephants, wild animals, flowers, and trees, and the [Traditional 18 Dances](/wiki/List_of_Indian_folk_dances "List of Indian folk dances") display the dancing of birds and animals.[[157]](#cite_note-157) For example:

* Mayura Wannama – The dance of the [peacock](/wiki/Peacock "Peacock")[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)
* Hanuma Wannama – The dance of the monkey[[160]](#cite_note-160)
* Gajaga Wannama – The dance of the elephant Musical types include:
* Local drama music includes [Kolam](/wiki/Kolam "Kolam")[[161]](#cite_note-161) and Nadagam types.[[162]](#cite_note-162) Kolam music is based on low country tunes primarily to accompany mask dance in [exorcism](/wiki/Exorcism "Exorcism") rituals.[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164) It is considered less developed/evolved, true to the folk tradition and a preserving of a more ancient artform.[[165]](#cite_note-165) It is limited to approximately 3–4 notes and is used by the ordinary people for pleasure and entertainment.[[166]](#cite_note-166)
* Nadagam music is a more developed form of drama influenced from South Indian street drama which was introduced by some south Indian artists. Phillippu Singho from Negombo in 1824 performed "Harishchandra Nadagama" in [Hnguranketha](/wiki/Hanguranketha_Divisional_Secretariat "Hanguranketha Divisional Secretariat") which was originally written in the [Telingu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") language. Later "Maname",[[167]](#cite_note-167) "Sanda kinduru"[[168]](#cite_note-168) and others were introduced. Don Bastian of [Dehiwala](/wiki/Dehiwala "Dehiwala") introduced Noorthy firstly by looking at Indian dramas and then [John de Silva](/wiki/John_de_Silva "John de Silva") developed it as did Ramayanaya in 1886.[[169]](#cite_note-169)
* Sinhala light music is currently the most popular type of music in Sri Lanka and enriched with the influence of folk music, kolam music, nadagam music, noorthy music, [film music](/wiki/Film_music "Film music"), classical music, Western music, and others.[[170]](#cite_note-170) Some artists visited India to learn music and later started introducing light music. [Ananda Samarakone](/wiki/Ananda_Samarakoon "Ananda Samarakoon") was the pioneer of this[[171]](#cite_note-171)[[172]](#cite_note-172) and also composed the national anthem.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

The classical Sinhalese orchestra consists of five categories of instruments, but among the percussion instruments, the drum is essential for dance.[[174]](#cite_note-174)
The vibrant beat of the rhythm of the drums form the basic of the dance.[[175]](#cite_note-175) The dancers' feet bounce off the floor and they leap and swirl in patterns that reflect the complex rhythms of the drum beat. This drum beat may seem simple on the first hearing but it takes a long time to master the intricate rhythms and variations, which the drummer sometimes can bring to a [crescendo](/wiki/Crescendo "Crescendo") of intensity. There are six common types of drums falling within 3 styles (one-faced, two-faced, and flat-faced):[[176]](#cite_note-176)[[177]](#cite_note-177)

* The typical Sinhala Dance is identified as the [Kandyan](/wiki/Kandy "Kandy") dance and the [Gatabera](/wiki/Geta_Bera "Geta Bera") drum is indispensable to this dance.[[178]](#cite_note-178)
* Yak-bera is the demon drum or the drum used in low country dance in which the dancers wear masks and perform devil dancing, which has become a highly developed form of art.[[179]](#cite_note-179)
* The Daula is a [barrel](/wiki/Barrel "Barrel")-shaped drum, and it was used as a companion drum with a Thammattama in the past, to keep strict time with the beat.[[180]](#cite_note-180)
* The [Thammattama](/wiki/Thammattama "Thammattama") is a flat, two-faced drum. The drummer strikes the drum on the two surfaces on top with sticks, unlike the others where you drum on the sides. This is a companion drum to the aforementioned Dawula.[[181]](#cite_note-181)
* A small double-headed hand drum is used to accompany songs. It is primarily heard in the poetry dances like vannam.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]
* The Rabana is a flat-faced circular drum and comes in several sizes.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The large Rabana – called the Banku Rabana – has to be placed on the floor like a circular short-legged [table](/wiki/Table_(furniture) "Table (furniture)") and several people (traditionally women) can sit around it and beat on it with both hands.[[183]](#cite_note-183) This is used in festivals such as the [Sinhalese New Year](/wiki/Sinhalese_New_Year "Sinhalese New Year") and ceremonies such as weddings.[[184]](#cite_note-184) The resounding beat of the Rabana symbolizes the joyous moods of the occasion. The small Rabana is a form of mobile drum beat since the player carries it wherever the person goes.[[185]](#cite_note-185)

Other instruments include:

* The Thalampata – 2 small cymbals joined by a string.[[186]](#cite_note-186)
* The wind section, is dominated by an instrument akin to the clarinet.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] This is not normally used for dances. This is important to note because the Sinhalese dance is not set to music as the western world knows it; rhythm is king.
* The flutes of metal such as silver & brass produce [shrill](/wiki/Shrillness "Shrillness") music to accompany Kandyan Dances, while the plaintive strains of music of the reed flute may pierce the air in devil-dancing. The [conch-shell](/wiki/Conch-shell "Conch-shell") ([Hakgediya](/wiki/Hakgediya "Hakgediya")) is another form of a natural instrument, and the player blows it to announce the opening of ceremonies of grandeur.[[187]](#cite_note-187)
* The [Ravanahatha](/wiki/Ravanahatha "Ravanahatha") (ravanhatta, rawanhattha, ravanastron or ravana hasta veena) is a bowed fiddle that was once popular in Western India.[[188]](#cite_note-188)[[189]](#cite_note-189) It is believed to have originated among the Hela civilisation of Sri Lanka in the time of King Ravana.[[190]](#cite_note-FSL-190) The bowl is made of cut coconut shell, the mouth of which is covered with goat hide. A dandi, made of bamboo, is attached to this shell.[[190]](#cite_note-FSL-190) The principal strings are two: one of steel and the other of a set of horsehair. The long bow has jingle bells[[191]](#cite_note-191)[[192]](#cite_note-dailynews.lk-192)

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Australia")]

See also: [Australian folk music](/wiki/Australian_folk_music "Australian folk music") and [Indigenous music of Australia](/wiki/Indigenous_music_of_Australia "Indigenous music of Australia")

[Indigenous Australian music](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian_music "Indigenous Australian music") includes the music of [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders"), who are collectively called [Indigenous Australians](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians");[[193]](#cite_note-193) it incorporates a variety of distinctive traditional music styles practiced by Indigenous Australian peoples, as well as a range of contemporary musical styles of and [fusion](/wiki/Fusion_(music) "Fusion (music)") with European traditions as interpreted and performed by indigenous Australian artists.[[194]](#cite_note-194) Music has formed an integral part of the [social](/wiki/Society "Society"), [cultural](/wiki/Culture "Culture") and ceremonial observances of these peoples, down through the millennia of their individual and collective histories to the present day.[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196) The traditional forms include many aspects of performance and [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument") unique to particular regions or [Indigenous Australian groups](/wiki/List_of_Indigenous_Australian_group_names "List of Indigenous Australian group names").[[197]](#cite_note-197) Equal elements of musical tradition are common through much of the Australian continent, and even beyond.[[198]](#cite_note-198) The culture of the Torres Strait Islanders is related to that of adjacent parts of [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") and so their music is also related. Music is a vital part of Indigenous Australians' cultural maintenance.[[199]](#cite_note-199)

Folk song traditions were taken to Australia by early settlers from England, Scotland and Ireland and gained particular foothold in the rural [outback](/wiki/Outback "Outback").[[200]](#cite_note-200)[[201]](#cite_note-201) The rhyming songs, poems and tales written in the form of [bush ballads](/wiki/Bush_ballad "Bush ballad") often relate to the itinerant and rebellious spirit of Australia in [The Bush](/wiki/The_Bush "The Bush"), and the authors and performers are often referred to as bush bards.[[202]](#cite_note-abc.net.au-202) The 19th century was the golden age of bush ballads.[[203]](#cite_note-203) Several collectors have catalogued the songs including [John Meredith](/wiki/John_Meredith_(folklorist) "John Meredith (folklorist)") whose recording in the 1950s became the basis of the collection in the [National Library of Australia](/wiki/National_Library_of_Australia "National Library of Australia").[[202]](#cite_note-abc.net.au-202)

The songs tell personal stories of life in the wide open country of Australia.[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205) Typical subjects include mining, raising and droving cattle, [sheep shearing](/wiki/Sheep_shearing "Sheep shearing"), wanderings, war stories, the [1891 Australian shearers' strike](/wiki/1891_Australian_shearers%27_strike "1891 Australian shearers' strike"), class conflicts between the landless working class and the [squatters](/wiki/Squatting_(pastoral) "Squatting (pastoral)") (landowners), and outlaws such as [Ned Kelly](/wiki/Ned_Kelly "Ned Kelly"), as well as love interests and more modern fare such as [trucking](/wiki/Truck_driver "Truck driver").[[206]](#cite_note-206) The most famous bush ballad is "[Waltzing Matilda](/wiki/Waltzing_Matilda "Waltzing Matilda")", which has been called "the unofficial national anthem of Australia".[[207]](#cite_note-207)
