##### Celtic traditional music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Celtic traditional music")]

See also: [Irish traditional music](/wiki/Irish_traditional_music "Irish traditional music") and [Scottish folk music](/wiki/Scottish_folk_music "Scottish folk music")

[Celtic music](/wiki/Celtic_music "Celtic music") is a term used by artists, record companies, music stores and music magazines to describe a broad grouping of [musical genres](/wiki/Musical_genre "Musical genre") that evolved out of the folk musical traditions of the [Celtic peoples](/wiki/Celtic_people "Celtic people").[[208]](#cite_note-208) These traditions include [Irish](/wiki/Folk_music_of_Ireland "Folk music of Ireland"), [Scottish](/wiki/Music_of_Scotland "Music of Scotland"), [Manx](/wiki/Music_of_the_Isle_of_Man "Music of the Isle of Man"), [Cornish](/wiki/Music_of_Cornwall "Music of Cornwall"), [Welsh](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales"), and [Breton](/wiki/Breton_music "Breton music") traditions.[[209]](#cite_note-209) [Asturian and Galician music](/wiki/Music_of_Galicia,_Cantabria_and_Asturias "Music of Galicia, Cantabria and Asturias") is often included, though there is no significant research showing that this has any close musical relationship.[[210]](#cite_note-210)[[211]](#cite_note-211) [Brittany](/wiki/Music_of_Brittany "Music of Brittany")'s Folk revival began in the 1950s with the "bagadoù" and the "kan-ha-diskan" before growing to world fame through [Alan Stivell](/wiki/Alan_Stivell "Alan Stivell")'s work since the mid-1960s.[[212]](#cite_note-saw-212)

In Ireland, [The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem](/wiki/The_Clancy_Brothers_and_Tommy_Makem "The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem") (although its members were all Irish-born, the group became famous while based in New York's Greenwich Village[[213]](#cite_note-213)), [The Dubliners](/wiki/The_Dubliners "The Dubliners"),[[214]](#cite_note-214) [Clannad](/wiki/Clannad_(musical_group) "Clannad (musical group)"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Planxty](/wiki/Planxty "Planxty"),[[215]](#cite_note-215) [The Chieftains](/wiki/The_Chieftains "The Chieftains"),[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217) [The Pogues](/wiki/The_Pogues "The Pogues"),[[218]](#cite_note-218) [The Corrs](/wiki/The_Corrs "The Corrs"),[[219]](#cite_note-219) [The Irish Rovers](/wiki/The_Irish_Rovers "The Irish Rovers"),[[220]](#cite_note-220) and a variety of other folk bands have done much over the past few decades to revitalise and re-popularise [Irish traditional music](/wiki/Folk_music_of_Ireland "Folk music of Ireland").[[221]](#cite_note-221) These bands were rooted, to a greater or lesser extent, in a tradition of Irish music and benefited from the efforts of artists such as [Seamus Ennis](/wiki/Seamus_Ennis "Seamus Ennis") and [Peter Kennedy](/wiki/Peter_Douglas_Kennedy "Peter Douglas Kennedy").[[212]](#cite_note-saw-212)

In [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), [The Corries](/wiki/The_Corries "The Corries"),[[222]](#cite_note-222) [Silly Wizard](/wiki/Silly_Wizard "Silly Wizard"),[[223]](#cite_note-223)[[224]](#cite_note-224) [Capercaillie](/wiki/Capercaillie_(band) "Capercaillie (band)"),[[225]](#cite_note-225) [Runrig](/wiki/Runrig "Runrig"),[[226]](#cite_note-226) [Jackie Leven](/wiki/Jackie_Leven "Jackie Leven"),[[227]](#cite_note-227) [Julie Fowlis](/wiki/Julie_Fowlis "Julie Fowlis"),[[228]](#cite_note-228) [Karine Polwart](/wiki/Karine_Polwart "Karine Polwart"),[[229]](#cite_note-229) [Alasdair Roberts](/wiki/Alasdair_Roberts_(musician) "Alasdair Roberts (musician)"),[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-231) [Dick Gaughan](/wiki/Dick_Gaughan "Dick Gaughan"),[[232]](#cite_note-232) [Wolfstone](/wiki/Wolfstone "Wolfstone"),[[233]](#cite_note-233) [Boys of the Lough](/wiki/Boys_of_the_Lough "Boys of the Lough"),[[234]](#cite_note-234) and [The Silencers](/wiki/The_Silencers_(band) "The Silencers (band)")[[235]](#cite_note-235) have kept Scottish folk vibrant and fresh by mixing traditional Scottish and Gaelic folk songs with more contemporary genres.[[236]](#cite_note-236) These artists have also been commercially successful in continental Europe and North America.[[237]](#cite_note-237) There is an emerging wealth of talent in the Scottish traditional music scene, with bands such as [Mànran](/wiki/M%C3%A0nran "Mànran"),[[238]](#cite_note-238) [Skipinnish](/wiki/Skipinnish "Skipinnish"),[[239]](#cite_note-239) Barluath[[240]](#cite_note-240) and [Breabach](/wiki/Breabach "Breabach")[[241]](#cite_note-241) and solo artists such as Patsy Reid,[[242]](#cite_note-242) [Robyn Stapleton](/wiki/Robyn_Stapleton "Robyn Stapleton")[[243]](#cite_note-243) and Mischa MacPherson[[244]](#cite_note-244) gaining a lot of success in recent years.[[245]](#cite_note-245)

##### Central and Eastern Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Central and Eastern Europe")]

See also: [Music of Belarus](/wiki/Music_of_Belarus "Music of Belarus"), [Hungarian folk music](/wiki/Hungarian_folk_music "Hungarian folk music"), [Music of Moldova](/wiki/Music_of_Moldova "Music of Moldova"), [Russian folk music](/wiki/Russian_folk_music "Russian folk music"), [Ukrainian folk music](/wiki/Ukrainian_folk_music "Ukrainian folk music"), [Music of Slovenia](/wiki/Music_of_Slovenia "Music of Slovenia"), and [Czech folklore](/wiki/Czech_folklore "Czech folklore")

During the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") era, national folk dancing was actively promoted by the state.[[246]](#cite_note-246) Dance troupes from Russia and Poland toured non-communist Europe from about 1937 to 1990.[[247]](#cite_note-247) The [Red Army Choir](/wiki/Alexandrov_Ensemble "Alexandrov Ensemble") recorded many albums, becoming the most popular military band.[[248]](#cite_note-248) Eastern Europe is also the origin of the Jewish [Klezmer](/wiki/Klezmer "Klezmer") tradition.[[249]](#cite_note-249)

Ľubomír Párička playing bagpipes, Slovakia

The [polka](/wiki/Polka "Polka") is a central European dance and also a [genre](/wiki/Musical_genre "Musical genre") of [dance music](/wiki/Dancing_music "Dancing music") familiar throughout Europe and the Americas. It originated in the middle of the 19th century in [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia").[[250]](#cite_note-250) Polka is still a popular genre of folk music in many European countries and is performed by folk artists in [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic"), [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"), [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), Germany, [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), Switzerland, Italy, [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"), Russia and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia").[[251]](#cite_note-251) Local varieties of this dance are also found in the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), United Kingdom, [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), Latin America (especially Mexico), and in the United States.

German [Volkslieder](/wiki/Lied "Lied") perpetuated by [Liederhandschriften](/wiki/Liederhandschrift "Liederhandschrift") manuscripts like *[Carmina Burana](/wiki/Carmina_Burana "Carmina Burana")*[[252]](#cite_note-252) date back to medieval [Minnesang](/wiki/Minnesang "Minnesang") and [Meistersinger](/wiki/Meistersinger "Meistersinger") traditions.[[253]](#cite_note-253) Those folk songs revived in the late 18th century period of [German Romanticism](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism"),[[254]](#cite_note-254) first promoted by [Johann Gottfried Herder](/wiki/Johann_Gottfried_Herder "Johann Gottfried Herder")[[255]](#cite_note-255)[[256]](#cite_note-256) and other advocates of the [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"),[[257]](#cite_note-257) later compiled by [Achim von Arnim](/wiki/Achim_von_Arnim "Achim von Arnim")[[258]](#cite_note-258) and [Clemens Brentano](/wiki/Clemens_Brentano "Clemens Brentano") (*[Des Knaben Wunderhorn](/wiki/Des_Knaben_Wunderhorn "Des Knaben Wunderhorn")*)[[259]](#cite_note-259) as well as by [Ludwig Uhland](/wiki/Ludwig_Uhland "Ludwig Uhland").[[260]](#cite_note-260)

The [Volksmusik](/wiki/Volksmusik "Volksmusik") and [folk dances](/wiki/Folk_dance "Folk dance") genre, especially in the [Alpine](/wiki/Alps "Alps") regions of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), Switzerland (*[Kuhreihen](/wiki/Ranz_des_Vaches "Ranz des Vaches")*) and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), up to today has lingered in rustic communities against the backdrop of industrialisation[[261]](#cite_note-261)—Low German [shanties](/wiki/Sea_shanty "Sea shanty") or the [Wienerlied](/wiki/Wienerlied "Wienerlied")[[262]](#cite_note-262) (*[Schrammelmusik](/wiki/Schrammelmusik "Schrammelmusik")*) being notable exceptions. [Slovene](/wiki/Music_of_Slovenia "Music of Slovenia") folk music in [Upper Carniola](/wiki/Upper_Carniola "Upper Carniola") and [Styria](/wiki/Styria_(Slovenia) "Styria (Slovenia)") also originated from the Alpine traditions, like the prolific Lojze Slak Ensemble.[[263]](#cite_note-263) Traditional *Volksmusik* is not to be confused with commercial *[Volkstümliche Musik](/wiki/Volkst%C3%BCmliche_Musik "Volkstümliche Musik")*, which is a derivation of that.[[264]](#cite_note-264)

The Hungarian group [Muzsikás](/wiki/Muzsik%C3%A1s "Muzsikás") played numerous American tours[[265]](#cite_note-265) and participated in the Hollywood movie *[The English Patient](/wiki/The_English_Patient_(film) "The English Patient (film)")*[[266]](#cite_note-266) while the singer [Márta Sebestyén](/wiki/M%C3%A1rta_Sebesty%C3%A9n "Márta Sebestyén") worked with the band [Deep Forest](/wiki/Deep_Forest "Deep Forest").[[267]](#cite_note-267) The Hungarian *[táncház](/wiki/T%C3%A1nch%C3%A1z "Táncház")* movement, started in the 1970s, involves strong cooperation between musicology experts and enthusiastic amateurs.[[268]](#cite_note-268) However, traditional Hungarian folk music and folk culture barely survived in some rural areas of Hungary, and it has also begun to disappear among the [ethnic Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians_in_Romania "Hungarians in Romania") in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"). The táncház movement revived broader folk traditions of music, dance, and costume together and created a new kind of music club.[[269]](#cite_note-HU-269) The movement spread to ethnic Hungarian communities elsewhere in the world.[[269]](#cite_note-HU-269)

##### Balkan music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Balkan music")]

Main article: [Balkan music](/wiki/Balkan_music "Balkan music")

See also: [Balkan folk music](/wiki/Balkan_folk_music "Balkan folk music") and [Romani music](/wiki/Romani_music "Romani music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Misteriata-na-balgarskite-glasove.jpg/250px-Misteriata-na-balgarskite-glasove.jpg)](/wiki/File:Misteriata-na-balgarskite-glasove.jpg)

[The Mystery of the Bulgarian Voices](/wiki/Bulgarian_State_Television_Female_Vocal_Choir "Bulgarian State Television Female Vocal Choir")

Balkan folk music was influenced by the mingling of Balkan ethnic groups in the period of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[270]](#cite_note-270) It comprises the [music of Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnian_folk_music "Bosnian folk music"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_folk_music "Bulgarian folk music"), [Croatia](/wiki/Croatian_folk_music "Croatian folk music"), [Greece](/wiki/Greek_folk_music "Greek folk music"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Culture_of_Montenegro#Traditional_music "Culture of Montenegro"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbian_folk_music "Serbian folk music"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_folk_music "Romanian folk music"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/Music_of_North_Macedonia#Folk_music "Music of North Macedonia"), [Albania](/wiki/Albanian_folk_music "Albanian folk music"), some of the historical states of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Music_of_Yugoslavia "Music of Yugoslavia") or [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Music_of_Serbia_and_Montenegro "Music of Serbia and Montenegro") and geographical regions such as [Thrace](/wiki/Music_of_Thrace "Music of Thrace").[[271]](#cite_note-271) Some music is characterised by complex rhythm.[[272]](#cite_note-272)

A notable act is [the Mystery of the Bulgarian Voices](/wiki/Bulgarian_State_Television_Female_Vocal_Choir "Bulgarian State Television Female Vocal Choir"), which won the [Grammy Award for Best Traditional Folk Recording](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Traditional_Folk_Album "Grammy Award for Best Traditional Folk Album") at the [32nd annual ceremony](/wiki/32nd_Annual_Grammy_Awards "32nd Annual Grammy Awards").[[273]](#cite_note-273)[[274]](#cite_note-274)

An important part of the whole Balkan folk music is the music of the local [Romani](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") ethnic minority, which is called [tallava](/wiki/Tallava "Tallava") and [brass band](/wiki/Brass_band "Brass band") music.[[275]](#cite_note-275)[[276]](#cite_note-276)

##### Nordic folk music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Nordic folk music")]

Main article: [Nordic folk music](/wiki/Nordic_folk_music "Nordic folk music")

See also: [Traditional Nordic dance music](/wiki/Traditional_Nordic_dance_music "Traditional Nordic dance music"), [Joik](/wiki/Joik "Joik"), [Nyckelharpa](/wiki/Nyckelharpa "Nyckelharpa"), and [Baltic psaltery](/wiki/Baltic_psaltery "Baltic psaltery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Latviu_senovinio_folkloro_grupe_Vilki.2009-08-22.jpg/250px-Latviu_senovinio_folkloro_grupe_Vilki.2009-08-22.jpg)](/wiki/File:Latviu_senovinio_folkloro_grupe_Vilki.2009-08-22.jpg)

Latvian men's folk ensemble [Vilki](/w/index.php?title=Vilki_(ensemble)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vilki (ensemble) (page does not exist)") [[lv](https://lv.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vilki_(folkloras_kopa) "lv:Vilki (folkloras kopa)")], performing at the festival of [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_people "Baltic people") [crafts](/wiki/Crafts "Crafts") and [warfare](/wiki/Warfare "Warfare") [Apuolė 854](/w/index.php?title=Apuol%C4%97_854&action=edit&redlink=1 "Apuolė 854 (page does not exist)") [[lt](https://lt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apuol%C4%97_854 "lt:Apuolė 854")] in [Apuolė Hillfort](/wiki/Apuol%C4%97_Hillfort "Apuolė Hillfort") on August 2009

Nordic folk music includes a number of traditions in Northern European, especially Scandinavian, countries. The [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") are generally taken to include [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), Sweden, [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland").[[277]](#cite_note-277) Sometimes it is taken to include the [Baltic countries](/wiki/Baltic_countries "Baltic countries") of [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania").[[278]](#cite_note-278)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Maria_Gasolina_Etnofestivaalit_2008.jpg/250px-Maria_Gasolina_Etnofestivaalit_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maria_Gasolina_Etnofestivaalit_2008.jpg)

Maria Gasolina performing at 2008 [Faces Festival](/wiki/Faces_(festival) "Faces (festival)") in [Raseborg](/wiki/Raseborg "Raseborg"), Finland

The many regions of the Nordic countries share certain traditions, many of which have diverged significantly, like Psalmodicon of Denmark, Sweden, and Norway.[[279]](#cite_note-279) It is possible to group together the Baltic states (or, sometimes, only Estonia) and parts of northwest Russia as sharing cultural similarities,[[280]](#cite_note-280) although the relationship has gone cold in recent years.[[281]](#cite_note-281) Contrast with Norway, Sweden, Denmark and the Atlantic islands of Iceland and the Faroe Islands, which share virtually no similarities of that kind. Greenland's Inuit culture has its own unique musical traditions.[[282]](#cite_note-282) Finland shares many cultural similarities with both the Baltic nations and the Scandinavian nations. The Sami of Sweden, Norway, Finland and Russia have their own unique culture, with ties to the neighboring cultures.[[283]](#cite_note-283)

[Swedish folk music](/wiki/Swedish_folk_music "Swedish folk music") is a [genre](/wiki/Musical_genre "Musical genre") of music based largely on [folkloric](/wiki/Folkloristics "Folkloristics") collection work that began in the early 19th century in Sweden.[[284]](#cite_note-284) The primary instrument of Swedish folk music is the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle").[[285]](#cite_note-285) Another common [instrument](/wiki/Musical_instrument "Musical instrument"), unique to Swedish traditions, is the [nyckelharpa](/wiki/Nyckelharpa "Nyckelharpa").[[286]](#cite_note-286) Most Swedish instrumental folk music is [dance music](/wiki/Dance_music "Dance music"); the signature music and dance form within Swedish folk music is the [polska](/wiki/Polska_(dance) "Polska (dance)").[[287]](#cite_note-287) Vocal and instrumental traditions in Sweden have tended to share [tunes](#Tune) historically, though they have been performed separately.[[288]](#cite_note-288) Beginning with the [folk music revival](/wiki/Folk_music_revival "Folk music revival") of the 1970s, vocalists and instrumentalists have also begun to perform together in folk music [ensembles](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble").

#### Latin America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Latin America")]

See also: [Andean music](/wiki/Andean_music "Andean music"), [Cueca](/wiki/Cueca "Cueca"), and [Nueva canción](/wiki/Nueva_canci%C3%B3n "Nueva canción")

The folk music of the Americas consists of the encounter and union of three main musical types: European traditional music, traditional music of the American natives, and tribal African music that arrived with slaves from that continent.

The particular case of Latin and South American music points to [Andean music](/wiki/Andean_music "Andean music")[[289]](#cite_note-289) among other native musical styles (such as Caribbean[[290]](#cite_note-290) and pampean), Iberian music of [Spain](/wiki/Music_of_Spain "Music of Spain") and [Portugal](/wiki/Music_of_Portugal "Music of Portugal"), and generally speaking [African](/wiki/Music_of_Africa "Music of Africa") tribal music, the three of which fused together evolving in differentiated musical forms in Central and South America.

[Andean music](/wiki/Andean_music "Andean music") comes from the region of the [Quechuas](/wiki/Quechua_people "Quechua people"), [Aymaras](/wiki/Aymara_people "Aymara people"), and other peoples that inhabit the general area of the [Inca Empire](/wiki/Inca_Empire "Inca Empire") prior to European contact.[[291]](#cite_note-291) It includes folklore music of parts of [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"),[[292]](#cite_note-292) Chile, [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") and [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela"). Andean music is popular to different degrees across Latin America, having its core public in rural areas and among indigenous populations. The [Nueva Canción](/wiki/Nueva_Canci%C3%B3n "Nueva Canción") movement of the 1970s revived the genre across Latin America and brought it to places where it was unknown or forgotten.

[Nueva canción](/wiki/Nueva_canci%C3%B3n "Nueva canción") (Spanish for 'new song') is a movement and genre within [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American_music "Latin American music") and [Iberian](/wiki/Iberia "Iberia") folk music, folk-inspired music, and socially committed music. In some respects its development and role is similar to the second folk music revival in North America. This includes evolution of this new genre from traditional folk music, essentially contemporary folk music except that that English genre term is not commonly applied to it. Nueva cancion is recognized as having played a powerful role in the social upheavals in Portugal, Spain and Latin America during the 1970s and 1980s.

Nueva cancion first surfaced during the 1960s as "The Chilean New Song" in Chile. The musical style emerged shortly afterwards in Spain and areas of Latin America where it came to be known under similar names. Nueva canción renewed traditional Latin American folk music, and with its political lyrics it was soon associated with revolutionary movements, the Latin American [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"), [Liberation Theology](/wiki/Liberation_Theology "Liberation Theology"), [hippie](/wiki/Hippie "Hippie") and human rights movements. It would gain great popularity throughout Latin America, and it is regarded as a precursor to [Rock en español](/wiki/Rock_en_espa%C3%B1ol "Rock en español").

[Cueca](/wiki/Cueca "Cueca") is a family of musical styles and associated dances from Chile, [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") and [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru").

[Trova](/wiki/Trova "Trova") and [Son](/wiki/Son_(music) "Son (music)") are styles of traditional [Cuban music](/wiki/Cuban_music "Cuban music") originating in the province of Oriente that includes influences from Spanish song and dance, such as [Bolero](/wiki/Bolero "Bolero") and [contradanza](/wiki/Contradanza "Contradanza") as well as [Afro-Cuban](/wiki/Afro-Cuban "Afro-Cuban") rhythm and percussion elements.

[Moda de viola](/wiki/Moda_de_viola "Moda de viola") is the name designated to Brazilian folk music. It is often performed with a 6-string nylon acoustic guitar, but the most traditional instrument is the [viola caipira](/wiki/Viola_caipira "Viola caipira"). The songs basically detailed the difficulties of life of those who work in the country. The themes are usually associated with the land, animals, folklore, impossible love and separation. Although there are some upbeat songs, most of them are nostalgic and melancholic.

#### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: North America")]

See also: [List of North American folk music traditions](/wiki/List_of_North_American_folk_music_traditions "List of North American folk music traditions")

##### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Lumbermen_violin_and_sticks_1943.jpg/250px-Lumbermen_violin_and_sticks_1943.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lumbermen_violin_and_sticks_1943.jpg)

French-Canadian [lumberjacks](/wiki/Lumberjack "Lumberjack") playing the fiddle, with sticks for percussion, in a lumber camp in 1943

Main article: [music of Canadian cultures](/wiki/Music_of_Canadian_cultures "Music of Canadian cultures")

See also: [Canadian folk music](/wiki/Canadian_folk_music "Canadian folk music"), [Music of Canada](/wiki/Music_of_Canada "Music of Canada"), and [French-Canadian music](/wiki/French-Canadian_music "French-Canadian music")

Canada's traditional folk music is particularly diverse.[[293]](#cite_note-Canada1-293) Even prior to liberalizing its immigration laws in the 1960s, Canada was ethnically diverse with dozens of different Indigenous and European groups present. In terms of music, academics do not speak of a Canadian tradition, but rather ethnic traditions ([Acadian music](/wiki/Acadian_music "Acadian music"), [Irish-Canadian music](/wiki/Irish-Canadian_music "Irish-Canadian music"), [Blackfoot music](/wiki/Blackfoot_music "Blackfoot music"), [Innu music](/wiki/Innu_music "Innu music"), [Inuit music](/wiki/Inuit_music "Inuit music"), [Métis fiddle](/wiki/M%C3%A9tis_fiddle "Métis fiddle"), etc.) and later in Eastern Canada regional traditions ([Newfoundland music](/wiki/Music_of_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Music of Newfoundland and Labrador"), [Cape Breton fiddling](/wiki/Cape_Breton_fiddling "Cape Breton fiddling"), [Quebecois music](/wiki/Quebecois_music "Quebecois music"), etc.)

> Traditional folk music of European origin has been present in Canada since the arrival of the first French and British settlers in the 16th and 17th centuries....They fished the coastal waters and farmed the shores of what became Newfoundland, Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Prince Edward Island, and the St Lawrence River valley of Quebec.
>
> The fur trade and its [voyageurs](/wiki/Voyageurs "Voyageurs") brought this farther north and west into Canada; later lumbering operations and lumberjacks continued this process.
>
> Agrarian settlement in eastern and southern Ontario and western Quebec in the early 19th century established a favorable milieu for the survival of many Anglo-Canadian folksongs and broadside ballads from Great Britain and the US. Despite massive industrialization, folk music traditions have persisted in many areas until today. In the north of Ontario, a large Franco-Ontarian population kept folk music of French origin alive.
>
> Populous Acadian communities in the Atlantic provinces contributed their song variants to the huge corpus of folk music of French origin centred in the province of Quebec. A rich source of Anglo-Canadian folk music can be found in the Atlantic region, especially Newfoundland. Completing this mosaic of musical folklore is the Gaelic music of Scottish settlements, particularly in Cape Breton, and the hundreds of Irish songs whose presence in eastern Canada dates from the Irish famine of the 1840s, which forced the large migrations of Irish to North America.[[293]](#cite_note-Canada1-293)

"Knowledge of the [history of Canada](/wiki/History_of_Canada "History of Canada")", wrote Isabelle Mills in 1974, "is essential in understanding the mosaic of Canadian folk song. Part of this mosaic is supplied by the folk songs of Canada brought by European and Anglo-Saxon settlers to the new land."[[13]](#cite_note-Mills-13) She describes how the French colony at Québec brought French immigrants, followed before long by waves of immigrants from Great Britain, Germany, and other European countries, all bringing music from their homelands, some of which survives into the present day. Ethnographer and folklorist [Marius Barbeau](/wiki/Marius_Barbeau "Marius Barbeau") estimated that well over ten thousand French folk songs and their variants had been collected in Canada. Many of the older ones had by then died out in France.

Music as professionalized paid entertainment grew relatively slowly in Canada, especially remote rural areas, through the 19th and early 20th centuries. While in urban music clubs of the [dance hall](/wiki/Dance_hall "Dance hall")/[vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") variety became popular, followed by jazz, rural Canada remained mostly a land of traditional music. Yet when American radio networks began broadcasting into Canada in the 1920s and 1930s, the audience for Canadian traditional music progressively declined in favour of [American Nashville-style country music](/wiki/Nashville_sound "Nashville sound") and urban styles like jazz.

The [Americanization](/wiki/Americanization "Americanization") of Canadian music led the [Canadian Radio League](/wiki/Canadian_Radio_League "Canadian Radio League") to lobby for a national public broadcaster in the 1930s, eventually leading to the creation of the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") (CBC) in 1936. The CBC promoted Canadian music, including traditional music, on its radio and later television services, but the [mid-century craze for all things "modern"](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") led to the decline of folk music relative to rock and pop. Canada was however influenced by the [folk music revival](/wiki/American_folk_music_revival "American folk music revival") of the 1950s and 60s, when local venues such as the Montreal Folk Workshop, and other folk clubs and coffee houses across the country, became crucibles for emerging songwriters and performers as well as for interchange with artists visiting from abroad.

##### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: United States")]

See also: [American folk music](/wiki/American_folk_music "American folk music")

American traditional music is also called roots music. Roots music is a broad category of music including [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music"), [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music"), [old time music](/wiki/Old_time_music "Old time music"), [jug bands](/wiki/Jug_band "Jug band"), [Appalachian folk](/wiki/Appalachian_folk_music "Appalachian folk music"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun_music "Cajun music") and [Native American music](/wiki/Indigenous_music_of_North_America "Indigenous music of North America"). The music is considered American either because it is native to the United States or because it developed there, out of foreign origins, to such a degree that it struck [musicologists](/wiki/Musicologist "Musicologist") as something distinctly new. It is considered "roots music" because it served as the basis of music later developed in the United States, including [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), contemporary folk music, rhythm and blues, and [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"). Some of these genres are considered to be traditional folk music.

* [Cajun music](/wiki/Cajun_music "Cajun music"), an emblematic [music of Louisiana](/wiki/Music_of_Louisiana "Music of Louisiana"), is rooted in the [ballads](/wiki/Ballads "Ballads") of the French-speaking [Acadians](/wiki/Acadian "Acadian") of Canada. Cajun music is often mentioned in tandem with the [Creole](/wiki/Louisiana_Creole_people "Louisiana Creole people")-based, [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun "Cajun")-influenced [zydeco](/wiki/Zydeco "Zydeco") form, both of [Acadiana](/wiki/Acadiana "Acadiana") origin. These [French Louisiana](/wiki/French_Louisiana#Modern_French_Louisiana "French Louisiana") sounds have influenced [American popular music](/wiki/American_popular_music "American popular music") for many decades, especially [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), and have influenced pop culture through mass media, such as television commercials.
* [Appalachian music](/wiki/Appalachian_music "Appalachian music") is the traditional music of the region of [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") in the Eastern United States. It derives from various European and African influences, including English [ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad"), Irish and Scottish traditional music (especially [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle") music), hymns, and African-American [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"). First recorded in the 1920s, Appalachian musicians were a key influence on the early development of [Old-time music](/wiki/Old-time_music "Old-time music"), [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), and [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music"), and were an important part of the [American folk music revival](/wiki/American_folk_music_revival "American folk music revival"). Instruments typically used to perform Appalachian music include the [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), [American fiddle](/wiki/American_fiddle "American fiddle"), [fretted dulcimer](/wiki/Appalachian_dulcimer "Appalachian dulcimer"), and guitar.[[294]](#cite_note-olson-294)

:   Early recorded Appalachian musicians include [Fiddlin' John Carson](/wiki/Fiddlin%27_John_Carson "Fiddlin' John Carson"), [Henry Whitter](/wiki/Henry_Whitter "Henry Whitter"), [Bascom Lamar Lunsford](/wiki/Bascom_Lamar_Lunsford "Bascom Lamar Lunsford"), the [Carter Family](/wiki/Carter_Family "Carter Family"), [Clarence Ashley](/wiki/Clarence_Ashley "Clarence Ashley"), [Frank Proffitt](/wiki/Frank_Proffitt "Frank Proffitt"), and [Dock Boggs](/wiki/Dock_Boggs "Dock Boggs"), all of whom were initially recorded in the 1920s and 1930s. Several Appalachian musicians obtained renown during the folk revival of the 1950s and 1960s, including [Jean Ritchie](/wiki/Jean_Ritchie "Jean Ritchie"), [Roscoe Holcomb](/wiki/Roscoe_Holcomb "Roscoe Holcomb"), [Ola Belle Reed](/wiki/Ola_Belle_Reed "Ola Belle Reed"), [Lily May Ledford](/wiki/Lily_May_Ledford "Lily May Ledford"), and [Doc Watson](/wiki/Doc_Watson "Doc Watson"). Country and bluegrass artists such as [Loretta Lynn](/wiki/Loretta_Lynn "Loretta Lynn"), [Roy Acuff](/wiki/Roy_Acuff "Roy Acuff"), [Dolly Parton](/wiki/Dolly_Parton "Dolly Parton"), [Earl Scruggs](/wiki/Earl_Scruggs "Earl Scruggs"), [Chet Atkins](/wiki/Chet_Atkins "Chet Atkins"), and [Don Reno](/wiki/Don_Reno "Don Reno") were heavily influenced by traditional Appalachian music.[[294]](#cite_note-olson-294) Artists such as [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Dave Van Ronk](/wiki/Dave_Van_Ronk "Dave Van Ronk"), [Jerry Garcia](/wiki/Jerry_Garcia "Jerry Garcia"), and [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen") have performed Appalachian songs or rewritten versions of Appalachian songs.

* The [Carter Family](/wiki/Carter_Family "Carter Family") was a [traditional American folk music](/wiki/American_folk_music "American folk music") group that recorded between 1927 and 1956. Their music had a profound impact on [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music"), [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [Southern gospel](/wiki/Southern_gospel "Southern gospel"), [pop](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") and rock musicians. They were the first vocal group to become country music stars; a beginning of the divergence of country music from traditional folk music. Their recordings of such songs as "[Wabash Cannonball](/wiki/Wabash_Cannonball "Wabash Cannonball")" (1932), "[Will the Circle Be Unbroken](/wiki/Can_the_Circle_Be_Unbroken_(By_and_By) "Can the Circle Be Unbroken (By and By)")" (1935), "[Wildwood Flower](/wiki/Wildwood_Flower "Wildwood Flower")" (1928), and "[Keep On the Sunny Side](/wiki/Keep_On_the_Sunny_Side "Keep On the Sunny Side")" (1928) made them country standards.[[295]](#cite_note-295)
* [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") and southern US plains: Before recorded history [American Indians](/wiki/American_Indians_(U.S._Census) "American Indians (U.S. Census)") in this area used songs and instrumentation; music and dance remain the core of ceremonial and social activities.[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) "[Stomp dance](/wiki/Stomp_dance "Stomp dance")" remains at its core, a [call and response](/wiki/Call_and_response_(music) "Call and response (music)") form; instrumentation is provided by [rattles](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_beater) "Rattle (percussion beater)") or [shackles](/wiki/Shackle "Shackle") worn on the legs of women.[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) "Other southeastern nations have their own complexes of sacred and social songs, including those for [animal dances](/wiki/Animal_dance "Animal dance") and friendship dances, and songs that accompany [stickball](/wiki/Stickball "Stickball") games. Central to the music of the southern [Plains Indians](/wiki/Plains_Indians "Plains Indians") is the drum, which has been called the heartbeat of Plains Indian music. Most of that genre can be traced back to activities of hunting and warfare, upon which plains culture was based."[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) The drum is central to the music of the southern plains Indians. During the reservation period, they used music to relieve boredom. Neighbors gathered, exchanged and created songs and dances; this is a part of the roots of the modern intertribal [powwow](/wiki/Pow_wow "Pow wow"). Another common instrument is the [courting flute](/w/index.php?title=Courting_flute&action=edit&redlink=1 "Courting flute (page does not exist)").[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296)
* African-American folk music in the area has roots in [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") and emancipation. [Sacred music](/wiki/Sacred_music "Sacred music")—[a capella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") and instrumentally-accompanied—is at the heart of the tradition. Early spirituals framed Christian beliefs within native practices and were heavily influenced by the music and rhythms of Africa."[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) [Spirituals](/wiki/Spiritual_(music) "Spiritual (music)") are prominent, and often use a call and response pattern.[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) "[Gospel](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music") developed after the Civil War (1861–1865). It relied on biblical text for much of its direction, and the use of metaphors and imagery was common. Gospel is a "joyful noise", sometimes accompanied by instrumentation and almost always punctuated by hand clapping, toe tapping, and body movement."[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) "[Shape-note](/wiki/Shape_note "Shape note") or [Sacred Harp](/wiki/Sacred_Harp "Sacred Harp") singing developed in the early 19th century as a way for itinerant singing instructors to teach church songs in rural communities. They taught using song books in which [musical notations](/wiki/Musical_notation "Musical notation") of tones were represented by [geometric shapes](/wiki/Geometric_shape "Geometric shape") that were designed to associate a shape with its pitch. Sacred harp singing became popular in many Oklahoma rural communities, regardless of ethnicity."[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) Later the blues tradition developed, with roots in and parallels to sacred music.[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) Then jazz developed, born from a "blend of ragtime, gospel, and blues"[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296)
* Anglo-Scots-Irish music traditions gained a place in Oklahoma after the [Land Run of 1889](/wiki/Land_Rush_of_1889 "Land Rush of 1889"). Because of its size and portability, the [fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle") was the core of early Oklahoma [Anglo](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") music, but other instruments such as the guitar, [mandolin](/wiki/Mandolin "Mandolin"), [banjo](/wiki/Banjo "Banjo"), and [steel guitar](/wiki/Steel_guitar "Steel guitar") were added later. Various Oklahoma music traditions trace their roots to the British Isles, including [cowboy ballads](/wiki/Cowboy_Music "Cowboy Music"), [western swing](/wiki/Western_swing "Western swing"), and contemporary [country and western](/wiki/Country_and_western "Country and western")."[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) Other Europeans (such as [Bohemians](/wiki/Bohemians_(tribe) "Bohemians (tribe)") and Germans) settled in the late 19th century. Their social activities centered on community halls, "where local musicians played [polkas](/wiki/Polka "Polka") and [waltzes](/wiki/Waltz "Waltz") on the accordion, piano, and [brass instruments](/wiki/Brass_instrument "Brass instrument")".[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296)

:   Mexican immigrants began to reach Oklahoma in the 1870s, bringing beautiful [canciones](/wiki/Canci%C3%B3n "Canción") and [corridos](/wiki/Corrido "Corrido") love songs, waltzes, and ballads along with them. Like American Indian communities, each [rite of passage](/wiki/Rite_of_passage "Rite of passage") in Hispanic communities is accompanied by traditional music. The acoustic guitar, [string bass](/wiki/String_bass "String bass"), and violin provide the basic instrumentation for Mexican music, with [maracas](/wiki/Maraca "Maraca"), flute, [horns](/wiki/Horn_(instrument) "Horn (instrument)"), or sometimes [accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion") filling out the sound.[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296) Later, Asians contributed to the musical mix. "Ancient music and dance traditions from the temples and [courts of China](/wiki/Chinese_court_music "Chinese court music"), India, and Indonesia are preserved in Asian communities throughout the state, and popular song genres are continually layered on to these classical music forms"[[296]](#cite_note-OklahomaFolk-296)

## Folk music revivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Folk music revivals")]

Main article: [Roots revival](/wiki/Roots_revival "Roots revival")

> "It's self-perpetuating, regenerative. It's what you'd call a perennial American song. I don't think it needs a revival, resuscitation. It lives and flourishes. It really just needs people who are 18 years old to get exposed to it. But it will go on with or without them. The folk song is more powerful than anything on the radio, than anything that's released...It's that distillation of the voices that goes on for a long, long time, and that's what makes them strong."[[297]](#cite_note-297)

— [Ketch Secor](/wiki/Old_Crow_Medicine_Show "Old Crow Medicine Show")

"Folk music revival" refers to either a period of renewed interest in traditional folk music, or to an event or period which transforms it; the latter usually includes a social activism component. A prominent example of the former is the British folk revival of approximately 1890–1920. The most prominent and influential example of the latter – to such an extent that it is usually referred to as ["*the* folk music revival"](/wiki/American_folk_music_revival "American folk music revival") – is the folk revival of the mid-20th Century, centered in the English-speaking world, in particular the United States, which gave birth to [contemporary folk music](/wiki/Contemporary_folk_music "Contemporary folk music").[[298]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGilliland1969shows_18–19-298)

One earlier revival influenced Western classical music. Composers such as [Percy Grainger](/wiki/Percy_Grainger "Percy Grainger"), [Ralph Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Ralph_Vaughan_Williams "Ralph Vaughan Williams"), and [Béla Bartók](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Bart%C3%B3k "Béla Bartók") made field recordings or transcriptions of folk singers and musicians.

In Spain, [Isaac Albéniz](/wiki/Isaac_Alb%C3%A9niz "Isaac Albéniz") (1860–1909) produced piano works reflect his Spanish heritage, including the *[Suite Iberia](/w/index.php?title=Suite_Iberia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Suite Iberia (page does not exist)")* (1906–1909). [Enrique Granados](/wiki/Enrique_Granados "Enrique Granados") (1867–1918) composed *[zarzuela](/wiki/Zarzuela "Zarzuela")*, Spanish light opera, and *Danzas Españolas* – Spanish Dances. [Manuel de Falla](/wiki/Manuel_de_Falla "Manuel de Falla") (1876–1946) became interested in the [cante jondo](/wiki/Cante_jondo "Cante jondo") of Andalusian [flamenco](/wiki/Flamenco "Flamenco"), the influence of which can be strongly felt in many of his works, which include *[Nights in the Gardens of Spain](/wiki/Nights_in_the_Gardens_of_Spain "Nights in the Gardens of Spain")* and *Siete canciones populares españolas* ("Seven Spanish Folksongs", for voice and piano). Composers such as [Fernando Sor](/wiki/Fernando_Sor "Fernando Sor") and [Francisco Tarrega](/wiki/Francisco_Tarrega "Francisco Tarrega") established the guitar as Spain's national instrument. Modern Spanish folk artists abound ([Mil i Maria](/w/index.php?title=Mil_i_Maria&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mil i Maria (page does not exist)"), [Russian Red](/wiki/Russian_Red "Russian Red"), et al.) modernizing while respecting the traditions of their forebears.

Flamenco grew in popularity through the 20th century, as did northern styles such as the Celtic music of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)"). French classical composers, from [Bizet](/wiki/Georges_Bizet "Georges Bizet") to [Ravel](/wiki/Maurice_Ravel "Maurice Ravel"), also drew upon Spanish themes, and distinctive Spanish genres became universally recognized.

Folk music revivals or [roots revivals](/wiki/Roots_revival "Roots revival") also encompass a range of phenomena around the world where there is a renewed interest in traditional music. This is often by the young, often in the traditional music of their own country, and often included new incorporation of social awareness, causes, and evolutions of new music in the same style. [Nueva canción](/wiki/Nueva_canci%C3%B3n "Nueva canción"), a similar evolution of a new form of socially committed music occurred in several Spanish-speaking countries.

## Festivals

Main article: [List of folk festivals](/wiki/List_of_folk_festivals "List of folk festivals")

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: United States")]

It is sometimes claimed that the earliest United States folk music festival was the Mountain Dance and Folk Festival,[[299]](#cite_note-299)[[300]](#cite_note-300) 1928, in [Asheville, North Carolina](/wiki/Asheville,_North_Carolina "Asheville, North Carolina"), founded by [Bascom Lamar Lunsford](/wiki/Bascom_Lamar_Lunsford "Bascom Lamar Lunsford").[[301]](#cite_note-301) The [National Folk Festival (USA)](/wiki/National_Folk_Festival_(USA) "National Folk Festival (USA)") is an itinerant folk festival in the United States.[[302]](#cite_note-302) Since 1934, it has been run by the [National Council for the Traditional Arts](/wiki/National_Council_for_the_Traditional_Arts "National Council for the Traditional Arts") (NCTA) and has been presented in 26 communities around the nation.[[303]](#cite_note-303) After leaving some of these communities, the National Folk Festival has [spun off](/wiki/Spin-off_(media) "Spin-off (media)") several locally run folk festivals in its wake including the [Lowell Folk Festival](/wiki/Lowell_Folk_Festival "Lowell Folk Festival"),[[304]](#cite_note-304) the Richmond Folk Festival,[[305]](#cite_note-305) the [American Folk Festival](/wiki/American_Folk_Festival "American Folk Festival")[[306]](#cite_note-306) and, most recently, the Montana Folk Festival.[[307]](#cite_note-307)

The [Newport Folk Festival](/wiki/Newport_Folk_Festival "Newport Folk Festival") is an annual folk festival held near Newport, Rhode Island.[[308]](#cite_note-308) It ran most years from 1959 to 1970 and from 1985 to the present, with an attendance of approximately 10,000 people each year.[[309]](#cite_note-309)

The four-day [Philadelphia Folk Festival](/wiki/Philadelphia_Folk_Festival "Philadelphia Folk Festival") began in 1962.[[310]](#cite_note-310) It is sponsored by the non-profit Philadelphia Folksong Society.[[311]](#cite_note-311) The event hosts contemporary and traditional artists in genres including World/Fusion, Celtic, Singer-Songwriter, Folk Rock, Country, Klezmer, and Dance.[[312]](#cite_note-312)[[313]](#cite_note-313) It is held annually on the third weekend in August.[[314]](#cite_note-314) The event now hosts approximately 12,000 visitors, presenting bands on 6 stages.[[315]](#cite_note-315)

The [Feast of the Hunters' Moon](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Hunters%27_Moon "Feast of the Hunters' Moon") in Indiana draws approximately 60,000 visitors per year.[[316]](#cite_note-Gisler-316)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Sidmouth Festival began in 1954,[[317]](#cite_note-317) and Cambridge Folk Festival began in 1965.[[318]](#cite_note-318) The [Cambridge Folk Festival](/wiki/Cambridge_Folk_Festival "Cambridge Folk Festival") in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England is noted for having a very wide definition of who can be invited as folk musicians.[[319]](#cite_note-319) The "club tents" allow attendees to discover large numbers of unknown artists, who, for ten or 15 minutes each, present their work to the festival audience.[[320]](#cite_note-320)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Australia")]

The [National Folk Festival](/wiki/National_Folk_Festival_(Australia) "National Folk Festival (Australia)") is Australia's premier folk festival event and is attended by over 50,000 people.[[321]](#cite_note-321)[[322]](#cite_note-322) The [Woodford Folk Festival](/wiki/Woodford_Folk_Festival "Woodford Folk Festival") and [Port Fairy Folk Festival](/wiki/Port_Fairy_Folk_Festival "Port Fairy Folk Festival") are similarly amongst Australia's largest major annual events, attracting top international folk performers as well as many local artists.[[323]](#cite_note-323)[[324]](#cite_note-324)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Folk_music&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Canada")]

Stan Rogers is a lasting fixture of the Canadian folk festival [Summerfolk](/wiki/Summerfolk_Music_and_Crafts_Festival "Summerfolk Music and Crafts Festival"), held annually in [Owen Sound, Ontario](/wiki/Owen_Sound,_Ontario "Owen Sound, Ontario"), where the main stage and amphitheater are dedicated as the "Stan Rogers Memorial Canopy".[[325]](#cite_note-325) The festival is firmly fixed in tradition, with Rogers' song "[The Mary Ellen Carter](/wiki/The_Mary_Ellen_Carter "The Mary Ellen Carter")" being sung by all involved, including the audience and a medley of acts at the festival.[[326]](#cite_note-326)[[327]](#cite_note-327) [The Canmore Folk Music Festival](/wiki/The_Canmore_Folk_Music_Festival "The Canmore Folk Music Festival") is Alberta's longest running folk music festival.[[328]](#cite_note-Canmore-328)

There are multitudes of folk festivals across Canada. [Canso, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Canso,_Nova_Scotia "Canso, Nova Scotia"), has hosted the [Stan Rogers Folk Festival](/wiki/Stan_Rogers_Folk_Festival "Stan Rogers Folk Festival") the last weekend of July each year since 1997. The town of [Wolfville](/wiki/Wolfville "Wolfville") hosts the Deep Roots Music Festival in September of each year. The [Celtic Colours](/wiki/Celtic_Colours "Celtic Colours") Celtic music festival is held each fall around the time that tree leaves are changing their colours, on [Cape Breton Island](/wiki/Cape_Breton_Island "Cape Breton Island"), with venues across the island. [Lunenburg, Nova Scotia](/wiki/Lunenburg,_Nova_Scotia "Lunenburg, Nova Scotia") has hosted the Lunenburg Folk Harbour Festival since 1986.
